title: Fix AES MixColumns to use correct GF(2^8) multiplication (I1 accuracy fix)
description: aes-engine.ts:192-208 MixColumns formula only uses 2 of 4 bytes per output. Correct implementation requires xtime() for GF(2^8) multiplication by 2, then multiply-accumulate with [[2,3,1,1],[1,2,3,1],[1,1,2,3],[3,1,1,2]] matrix. Source: FIPS-197 §5.1.3. Implementation: add xtime(b) = ((b<<1)^(b&0x80?0x1b:0))&0xff, then out[0][c] = xtime(a0)^xtime(a1)^a1^a2^a3. NOTE: Consolidates SEC-019/SEC-099 — single authoritative fix task.
acceptanceCriteria: xtime() correctly implements GF(2^8) multiplication by 2 with 0x1b reduction
MixColumns output matches NIST FIPS-197 Appendix B known test vector
Each output byte of MixColumns depends on ALL 4 input bytes in column
Step descriptions updated to reflect correct mixing formula

title: Fix cert chain step description: 'HMAC' → 'RSA/ECDSA digital signature verification'
description: cert-chain.ts:184 says 'HMAC(serverCert, intermediateCA.publicKey)'. HMAC is symmetric; X.509 certs use asymmetric digital signatures. This is a visual lie — the text teaches a fundamentally wrong concept. Fix: change to 'RSA/ECDSA signature verification using intermediateCA.publicKey'. NOTE: Consolidates SEC-020/SEC-100.
acceptanceCriteria: Zero instances of 'HMAC' in cert-chain.ts step descriptions
Correctly describes asymmetric digital signature verification
Mentions that RSA/ECDSA signature uses the issuer's PUBLIC key to verify

title: Fix password hashing 'Blowfish-based' claim when using FNV-1a
description: password-hashing.ts:140 says 'the Blowfish-based key schedule' but simpleHash at line 48 uses FNV-1a. Either implement a simplified Blowfish-like key schedule (expensive, not necessary for teaching) OR change the description to accurately say 'a hash function' without falsely claiming Blowfish. The cost factor and intentional-slowness concept can be taught without claiming the wrong algorithm. NOTE: Consolidates SEC-101.
acceptanceCriteria: Description accurately reflects the hash function being used
No false 'Blowfish' claim if implementation doesn't use it
Cost factor / intentional slowness teaching preserved

title: Add AES cell-level diff highlighting to show WHICH bytes changed per sub-step
description: AES matrix visualization (SecurityModule.tsx:988-1010) colors ALL 16 cells the same color per sub-step. A student can't see which bytes changed. Add diff highlighting: after SubBytes, highlight cells whose value differs from the pre-SubBytes state. After ShiftRows, use arrows or animation to show which rows moved. After MixColumns, highlight entire columns that were mixed. After AddRoundKey, show XOR visually (old value ⊕ key = new value).
acceptanceCriteria: Cells that changed value have a different visual indicator (glow, border, or animation)
Unchanged cells are visually dimmed or neutral
ShiftRows shows directional movement (row sliding animation)
Student can identify WHICH cells were affected by each sub-step

title: Add DH edge case validation for p=0, p=1, and negative values
description: diffie-hellman.ts modPow(base, exp, 0) produces NaN. modPow with negative exp gives wrong results. Add input validation: p must be >= 2 (ideally prime), g must be >= 2 and < p, a and b must be >= 1. Show clear error message in UI instead of NaN. NOTE: Consolidates SEC-022.
acceptanceCriteria: p=0 shows error message, not NaN
p=1 shows warning about degenerate case
Negative values show validation error
Error appears in DH properties panel, not as broken visualization

title: Add AES parseHex validation to reject non-hex characters with clear error
description: aes-engine.ts:337-343 parseHex converts invalid input ('zz') to 0 silently via `parseInt(x,16)||0`. Student enters garbage and gets wrong output with no error. Add validation: reject chars outside [0-9a-fA-F ], show specific error naming the invalid characters. NOTE: Consolidates SEC-023.
acceptanceCriteria: Non-hex characters produce a visible error in AES properties panel
Error message names the specific invalid characters
Valid partial input (< 16 bytes) still works with zero-padding and info note

title: Add pseudocode panel to AES, DH, rate limiting, and CORS topics
description: I6 Pseudocode = 0/3 across ALL 13 engines. This is the single biggest systemic implementation gap. Start with 4 topics: AES (show SubBytes/ShiftRows/MixColumns/AddRoundKey in pseudocode that maps to the engine code 1:1), DH (show modPow and key exchange steps), Rate Limiting (show Token Bucket algorithm), CORS (show browser decision algorithm). Use language-agnostic pseudocode with numbered steps.
acceptanceCriteria: AES pseudocode shows the 4 sub-steps per round with loop structure
DH pseudocode shows modPow + exchange protocol
Rate Limiting pseudocode shows Token Bucket with refill logic
CORS pseudocode shows same-origin check → simple check → preflight → response validation
Pseudocode lines map 1:1 to engine code functions

title: Add Python display code for AES, DH, and JWT topics
description: I7 Multi-Language = 0/3 across all engines. Students want to see code in THEIR language. Start with Python (most popular teaching language) for 3 topics: AES (show SubBytes + ShiftRows as clean Python functions), DH (show modPow and exchange), JWT (show encode with base64url + hmac). Display code should match the TypeScript engine logic 1:1.
acceptanceCriteria: Python code panel available for AES, DH, and JWT
Python code logic matches TypeScript engine 1:1
Code is clean, commented, and beginner-readable
Copy-to-clipboard button on code panel

title: Add JWT RS256 variant alongside existing HS256
description: jwt-engine.ts only supports HS256 (symmetric HMAC). RS256 (asymmetric RSA) is used by most production systems. Add: (1) RS256 key pair generation (simplified/educational), (2) Signing with private key, (3) Verification with public key, (4) Comparison: 'HS256 = shared secret, RS256 = key pair. Use RS256 when the verifier shouldn't have signing ability.' This addresses JWT I2=1/3 and I8=1/3.
acceptanceCriteria: JWT Lifecycle has a toggle for HS256 vs RS256
RS256 mode shows public/private key pair
Signing uses private key, verification uses public key
Comparison text explains when to use each

title: Add AES mode comparison (ECB vs CBC vs GCM) with visual demo
description: AES engine shows only raw AES-128 block cipher with no mode of operation. In practice, AES is NEVER used without a mode. Add: ECB mode (show the penguin pattern vulnerability — same plaintext block = same ciphertext block), CBC mode (show chaining), GCM mode (show authenticated encryption). The ECB penguin is one of the most famous crypto teaching visuals. Source: Wikipedia 'Block cipher mode of operation'.
acceptanceCriteria: Mode selector: ECB, CBC, GCM
ECB mode visually shows identical plaintext blocks producing identical ciphertext
CBC mode shows initialization vector and chaining
GCM mode shows authentication tag generation
'ECB penguin' concept demonstrated (same-color blocks in ECB vs randomized in CBC)

title: Add ECDH (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman) variant alongside integer DH
description: DH engine uses only integer modular arithmetic. Modern TLS 1.3 uses x25519 (ECDH). Add: (1) Simplified elliptic curve visualization showing point addition, (2) ECDH key exchange using curve points instead of integers, (3) Comparison: 'x25519 achieves 128-bit security with 256-bit keys. Integer DH needs 3072-bit keys for the same security level.' Source: NIST SP 800-186.
acceptanceCriteria: DH topic has a toggle for Integer DH vs ECDH
ECDH shows point addition concept on a simplified curve
Key size comparison table between integer DH and ECDH
Mentions x25519 as TLS 1.3 default

title: Add HTTPS TLS 1.2 vs 1.3 comparison mode
description: HTTPS flow engine only shows TLS 1.3 (1-RTT). TLS 1.2 (2-RTT) is still widely used and the comparison teaches WHY 1.3 is better. Add: toggle for TLS 1.2 vs 1.3 showing the extra round-trip, the different handshake messages (ServerHelloDone vs EncryptedExtensions), and the security improvements (forward secrecy mandatory in 1.3).
acceptanceCriteria: Toggle for TLS 1.2 vs TLS 1.3 in HTTPS flow
TLS 1.2 shows 2-RTT handshake with additional messages
Side-by-side timing comparison showing latency difference
Security improvements of 1.3 highlighted (forward secrecy, fewer cipher suites)

title: Add stored XSS and DOM-based XSS variants to Web Attacks
description: Web Attacks only shows reflected XSS (user input echoed in response). Missing: Stored XSS (payload saved in database, executes when other users view the page — more dangerous) and DOM-based XSS (payload processed entirely client-side via document.location, innerHTML, etc.). Three XSS types is the standard curriculum. Source: OWASP XSS, PortSwigger XSS types.
acceptanceCriteria: XSS has sub-tabs: Reflected (existing), Stored (new), DOM-based (new)
Stored XSS shows payload persisting in database and affecting other users
DOM-based XSS shows client-side JavaScript processing without server involvement
Each type has both attack and defense flows

title: Add real-world production comparison for AES (WebCrypto API) and DH (TLS x25519)
description: I9 Real-World = 0/3 across all engines. For AES: show side-by-side our educational implementation vs WebCrypto API `crypto.subtle.encrypt('AES-GCM', ...)` — 'Our 351-line implementation runs 10 rounds manually. In production, this is one line of code because the browser does it in hardware (AES-NI instruction set).' For DH: 'Our modPow uses JavaScript numbers. TLS 1.3 uses x25519 with 256-bit integers. The math is the same; the numbers are astronomically larger.'
acceptanceCriteria: AES properties panel shows WebCrypto API code snippet
DH properties panel shows TLS x25519 usage note
'Teaching vs Production' comparison explains what differs and why
Student understands that the concept is the same but production uses hardware acceleration
